sample.head:
# Cellular Senescence in Aging: Mechanistic Controversies, Paradoxes, and Therapeutic Evidence

## Three Frameworks as a Temporally Ordered Cascade

The mechanistic debate over cellular senescence has largely resolved from a competition among rival models into a more nuanced argument about relative contributions across time and tissue context. The three canonical frameworks — DNA damage response, mitochondrial dysfunction, and chromatin remodeling — are now best understood as sequential and reinforcing modules in a single cascade rather than alternative explanations.

**The DNA Damage Response Model.** d'Adda di Fagagna et al. (2003) established that critically short telomeres generate persistent double-strand break foci (co-localizing γH2AX, 53BP1, and activated ATM/ATR) functionally indistinguishable from exogenous DNA damage, activating p53/p21 and independently engaging p16-INK4a/Rb to enforce irreversible G1 arrest. This framework explains replicative senescence with molecular precision and underpins the telomerase-deficient mouse model as a biological clock. Its limitation is that it provides no mechanistic account of why arrested cells generate an energetically costly inflammatory secretome, and it struggles to explain stress-induced or oncogene-induced senescence where telomeres remain intact.
